---
title: "Stories (ToM) — dimensionality & sub-construct structure"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Question

`stories_tom.html` found ToM is not cleanly unidimensional (ev1/ev2 ≈ 1.9).
Here we ask: **is there an interpretable sub-construct structure?** Do items
group by (a) the story-construct schemes, (b) question type, or (c) story
(testlet/dependency structure) — and would any sub-scale (e.g. false-belief
targets) be a cleaner developmental measure than the full composite?

Analysis is on the DE (Leipzig) sample — the largest and the only one with ToM
retest data. Items restricted to those with >40% coverage and non-extreme
difficulty (for tetrachoric stability). Hostile-attribution items excluded.

```{r setup}
source(here::here("common.R"))
library(psych); library(mirt)
meta <- read_rds(here::here("data/tom_meta/tom_item_metadata.rds")) |>
  filter(!is_hostile_attribution)
run_age <- read_rds(here::here("data/scores_all_sites.rds")) |>
  filter(task_id == "theory-of-mind") |> distinct(run_id, age)
tr <- load_levante_trials(task_ids = "theory-of-mind") |>
  filter(!str_starts(item_uid, "ha_")) |>
  left_join(run_age, by = "run_id") |>
  left_join(meta |> select(item_uid, tom_scenario, old_group, new_construct,
                           question_type, is_control), by = "item_uid") |>
  mutate(correct = as.numeric(correct))

de_mat <- tr |> filter(dataset == "pilot_mpieva_de_main") |>
  distinct(run_id, item_uid, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  select(run_id, item_uid, correct) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = item_uid, values_from = correct) |>
  select(-run_id) |> as.matrix()
de_mat <- de_mat[, colMeans(!is.na(de_mat)) > 0.4]
p <- colMeans(de_mat, na.rm = TRUE); de_mat <- de_mat[, p > 0.08 & p < 0.97]
tc <- suppressWarnings(tetrachoric(de_mat, smooth = TRUE)$rho)
```

# 1. How many dimensions?

```{r eigen}
ev <- eigen(tc)$values
pa <- suppressWarnings(fa.parallel(tc, n.obs = nrow(de_mat), fa = "fa", plot = FALSE))
tibble(component = 1:8, eigenvalue = round(ev[1:8], 2)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = glue("DE, {ncol(de_mat)} items. ev1/ev2 = {round(ev[1]/ev[2],2)}; parallel analysis suggests {pa$nfact} factors"))
```

```{r scree, fig.height=3.5}
tibble(k = seq_along(ev), ev = ev) |>
  ggplot(aes(k, ev)) + geom_line() + geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, lty = 3) +
  labs(x = "Component", y = "Eigenvalue", title = "Scree (tetrachoric)")
```

A shallow scree with no clear elbow and a parallel-analysis suggestion of many
factors is the signature of **diffuse, weakly-correlated items**, not a clean
low-dimensional structure. (The large "n factors" from parallel analysis is
over-extraction on a noisy sparse tetrachoric matrix — it should be read as
"no dominant structure," not "10 real factors.")

# 2. What organizes the (weak) covariance? Story vs question type vs construct

For every item pair, compare tetrachoric correlations within vs. between each
grouping. If a grouping is the real structure, within-group r should clearly
exceed between-group r.

```{r structure}
itmap <- meta |> filter(item_uid %in% colnames(tc)) |>
  select(item_uid, tom_scenario, question_type, old_group, new_construct)
pr <- which(upper.tri(tc), arr.ind = TRUE)
pd <- tibble(i = colnames(tc)[pr[,1]], j = colnames(tc)[pr[,2]], r = tc[upper.tri(tc)]) |>
  left_join(itmap, by = c("i"="item_uid")) |>
  rename(story_i=tom_scenario, qt_i=question_type, og_i=old_group, nc_i=new_construct) |>
  left_join(itmap, by = c("j"="item_uid")) |>
  rename(story_j=tom_scenario, qt_j=question_type, og_j=old_group, nc_j=new_construct)

bind_rows(
  pd |> transmute(grouping = "same story",         same = story_i == story_j, r),
  pd |> transmute(grouping = "same question type", same = qt_i == qt_j,       r),
  pd |> transmute(grouping = "same old group",     same = og_i == og_j,       r),
  pd |> transmute(grouping = "same new construct", same = nc_i == nc_j,       r)
) |>
  group_by(grouping, same) |>
  summarise(n_pairs = n(), mean_r = mean(r, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = same, values_from = c(n_pairs, mean_r)) |>
  mutate(within_minus_between = mean_r_TRUE - mean_r_FALSE) |>
  select(grouping, mean_r_within = mean_r_TRUE, mean_r_between = mean_r_FALSE,
         within_minus_between) |>
  arrange(desc(within_minus_between)) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Within- vs between-group item correlation")
```

**Question type is the strongest organizer** (within 0.20 vs between 0.11),
ahead of story testlet (0.17 vs 0.13) and well ahead of either construct scheme
(near zero difference). But note the absolute level: even within question type,
mean r ≈ 0.2 — the items are weakly correlated across the board.

```{r efa-loadings}
f2 <- suppressWarnings(fa(tc, nfactors = 2, n.obs = nrow(de_mat),
                          rotate = "oblimin", fm = "minres"))
as.data.frame(unclass(f2$loadings)) |> rownames_to_column("item_uid") |>
  left_join(meta |> select(item_uid, question_type), by = "item_uid") |>
  group_by(question_type) |>
  summarise(n = n(), MR1 = round(mean(MR1), 2), MR2 = round(mean(MR2), 2),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  knitr::kable(caption = glue("2-factor EFA mean loadings by question type (factor r = {round(f2$Phi[1,2],2)}; {round(sum(f2$Vaccounted['Proportion Var',])*100)}% variance)"))
```

A 2-factor solution doesn't cleanly separate question types onto distinct
factors (everything loads modestly on MR1), confirming there isn't a tidy
2–3 sub-construct structure to recover.

# 3. Would a sub-scale beat the full composite?

Compare the full item set against targets-only and false-belief-only on
unidimensionality, marginal reliability, and age validity (DE).

```{r subscale, cache=TRUE}
rel <- function(items) {
  m <- de_mat[, intersect(colnames(de_mat), items), drop = FALSE]
  m <- m[, apply(m, 2, \(x) length(unique(x[!is.na(x)])) > 1), drop = FALSE]
  m <- m[rowSums(!is.na(m)) > 0, , drop = FALSE]
  rho <- suppressWarnings(tetrachoric(m, smooth = TRUE)$rho)
  e <- eigen(rho)$values
  fit <- mirt(m, 1, itemtype = "Rasch", verbose = FALSE, technical = list(NCYCLES = 3000))
  tibble(n_items = ncol(m), ev1_ev2 = e[1]/e[2],
         marginal_reliability = as.numeric(marginal_rxx(fit)))
}
target_items <- meta |> filter(!is_control) |> pull(item_uid)
fb_items     <- meta |> filter(question_type == "false_belief") |> pull(item_uid)

psc <- tr |> filter(dataset == "pilot_mpieva_de_main", !is.na(age)) |>
  group_by(run_id, age) |>
  summarise(all = mean(correct),
            targets = mean(correct[!is_control]),
            fb = mean(correct[question_type == "false_belief"]), .groups = "drop")
agecor <- c(all = cor(psc$all, psc$age, use="complete.obs"),
            targets = cor(psc$targets, psc$age, use="complete.obs"),
            fb = cor(psc$fb, psc$age, use="complete.obs"))

bind_rows(
  rel(colnames(de_mat)) |> mutate(scale = "all items", age_validity = agecor["all"]),
  rel(target_items)     |> mutate(scale = "targets only", age_validity = agecor["targets"]),
  rel(fb_items)         |> mutate(scale = "false-belief only", age_validity = agecor["fb"])
) |> select(scale, n_items, ev1_ev2, marginal_reliability, age_validity) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Sub-scale comparison (DE)")
```

**The false-belief core scale does not pan out.** It is no more unidimensional
(ev1/ev2 ≈ 1.9, same as full), markedly *less* reliable (~0.62 vs 0.92, it has
far fewer items), and *no* more age-valid (r ≈ 0.40, same as full). Targets-only
is similar to all-items. So there is no sub-scale that buys a cleaner or more
valid measure than the full composite.

# Synthesis

- **ToM is a reliable but heterogeneous composite, not a clean unidimensional
  construct.** Inter-item correlations are uniformly low (~0.13); the full
  scale's reliability (0.92) comes from aggregating many weakly-related items.
- **No tidy sub-construct structure exists to recover.** Neither story-construct
  scheme organizes the covariance; question type does so slightly (within-type
  r ≈ 0.20) but not enough to define clean factors. The story-testlet
  (dependency) effect is real but weak.
- **Sub-scaling doesn't help.** A false-belief-only scale is less reliable and
  no more age-valid than the full composite.
- **Practical recommendation:** keep using the full-composite θ as an "overall
  social reasoning" score; don't over-interpret it as a pure construct, and
  don't bother carving sub-scales from this data. If a cleaner construct is the
  goal, it likely needs item/design revision (more items per dimension,
  reduced reliance on near-ceiling controls), not re-scoring.
- **Caveat:** DE-only, sparse (CAT-like) data limits factor-analytic precision;
  the multidimensionality is partly real (low item correlations) and partly
  noise. A bifactor / testlet IRT model would be the principled next step but is
  risky at this sample size.
